concepts.title: Ferrous Sulphate
concepts.conceptBody:
Compounds Of Iron

Ferrous Sulphate or Green vitriol $\left( \mathbf{FeS}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}\cdot \mathbf{7}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O} \right)$

The name green vitriol is given because of its green colour.

(i) On heating, green $\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot 7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ gives a white anhydrous salt. On strong heating it forms $\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}},\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$.

$\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot 7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{heating}\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

White

$2\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{heating}\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\uparrow $

Brown

(ii) With nitric oxide, ferrous sulphate turns brown due to the formation of nitroso ferrous sulphate$\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$. NO

concepts.title: Ferric Oxide
concepts.conceptBody:
Ferric Oxide $\mathbf{-F}{{\mathbf{e}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$ (Reddish Brown)

Preparation 

(i) In lab, it can be prepared by heating ferrous sulphate or ferric hydroxide.

$2\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\uparrow$

$2\text{Fe}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(ii) It is also obtained as a by product in the manufacture of sulphuric acid from iron pyrites.

$4\text{Fe}{{\text{S}}_{2}}+11{{\text{O}}_{2}}\to 2\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+8\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Properties: 

It is a reddish brown powder, insoluble in water but soluble in acid. Amphoteric in nature and reacts with acids and alkalies.

$\text{NaFe}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{Na}$

$\text{OHF}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+6\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

$\text{OF}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{NaFe}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Sodium ferrate

concepts.title: Ferric chloride and Ferrous Ammonium sulphate
concepts.conceptBody:
Ferric Chloride (Red Black)

Anhydrous ferric chloride,$\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{6}}$is obtained by heating iron filing in a stream of dry chlorine. The compound sublimes and collects in the form of deep red-black flaky crystals.

$2\text{Fe}+3\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\to \text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{6}}$

It dissociated on heating above 973 K first into $\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ and then into $\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$.

$\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{6}}\xrightarrow{973K}2\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }2\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow $

Its aq. solution is acidic in nature due to hydrolysis.

It is soluble in water, alcohol and ether and forms hydrates. The most common hydrate of iron $\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\cdot 6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is yellow in colour.

Ferrous Ammonium Sulphate (Mohr’s Salt), FeSO$_{\mathbf{4}}\cdot {{\left( \mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{4}}} \right)}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}\cdot \mathbf{6}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O}$ 

It is prepared by crystallising a solution containing equivalent amounts of iron (II) sulphate and ammonium sulphate. It is a pale green crystaline compound which does not effloresce like ferrous sulphate.

concepts.title: Cupric Chloride
concepts.conceptBody:
Compounds Of Copper

Cupric chloride, $\mathbf{CuC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{\times 2}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O}$ (Green Crystal) 

Preparation: 

\[2\text{Cu}+4\text{HCl}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{CuC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\]

\[\text{CuO}+2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CuC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\]

Properties: 

(i) It is deliquescent compound and is readily soluble in water. The dilute solution is blue but concentrated solution is, however, green. The blue colour is due to complex cation${{\left[ \text{Cu}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]}^{2+}}$

(ii) On heating forms$\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

$2\text{CuC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\to \text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

(iii) $\text{CuC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{Cu}\to \text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

$2\text{CuC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+2\text{H}\to \text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$$2\text{HCl}$

$2\text{CuC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{SnC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\to \text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{SnC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$

concepts.title: Copper Sulphate
concepts.conceptBody:
Copper Sulphate (Blue Vitriol), $\mathbf{CuS}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}\cdot \mathbf{5}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O}$

Preparation: 

(i) It is prepared by dissolving copper (II) oxide or copper (II) carbonate in dil. sulphuric acid.

$\text{CuO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{CuC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

On evaporation, solution is concentrated, blue crystals of $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}.5{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ separate out on cooling.

(ii) On a large scale, copper (II) sulphate is obtained by passing air through a hot mixture of copper and dil. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$.

$2\text{Cu}+4{{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Properties: 

(i) On heating$\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}.5{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ loses water molecules as follows.

$\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot 5{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow[Strong\,100{}^\circ C]{373K}\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$$\xrightarrow[150{}^\circ C]{423K}\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CuO}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

(ii) $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+6\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}\to \left[ \text{Cu}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}} \right]{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Schweitzer’s reagent

(iii) \[2\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4\text{KI}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}\downarrow +{{\text{I}}_{2}}\uparrow +2~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\].

A mixture of copper sulphate and lime, under the name of Bordeaux mixture is used as a fungicide in agriculture.

concepts.title: Cupric Oxide
concepts.conceptBody:
Cupric Oxide, CuO (Black)

It is called black oxide of copper.

Preparation: 

(i) It is prepared by heating following copper compounds:

$\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{O}+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{CuO}$

$2\text{Cu}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{CuO}$

$\text{Cu}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CuO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$2\text{Cu}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{CuO}+4\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

(ii) On commercial scale, it is obtained by heating malachite, which is found in nature.

$\text{CuC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\cdot \text{Cu}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{CuO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Properties: 

It is black powder, insoluble in water but dissolves in acids. It is reduced to metallic copper by reducing agents like $\text{H},\text{C}$ and $\text{Co}$.

$\text{CuO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Cu}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

concepts.title: Silver Nitrate
concepts.conceptBody:
Compounds of Silver

Silver Nitrate, $\mathbf{AgN}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$ (Lunar Caustic) 

Preparation: 

$3\text{Ag}+\underset{\text{(Dilute)}}{4\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}\xrightarrow{Heat}3\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{NO}\uparrow $

Properties: 

(i) $2\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{Heat}2\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

\(2 \mathrm{AgNO}_3+\xrightarrow{\text { red Heat }} 2 \mathrm{Ag}+2 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2\)

(ii) It reacts with iodine in two ways.

(a) When iodine is in excess

$5\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3{{\text{I}}_{2}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{HI}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+5\text{AgI}+5\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

(b) When$\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is in excess

$6\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3{{\text{I}}_{2}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{AgI}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+5\text{AgI}+6\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

(iii) When treated with alkali, it gives precipitate of silver oxide, which dissolves in excess of$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}$

$2\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+2\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{O}\downarrow +2\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

brown ppt

$2\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{O}\downarrow +2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{O}+4\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}\xrightarrow{{}}2\left[ \text{Ag}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}} \right]\text{OH}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(iv) $2\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+2\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

$\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{3}}\left[ \text{Ag}{{\left( {{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}} \right.$

concepts.title: Silver Bromide
concepts.conceptBody:
Silver Bromide, AgBr

Preparation: 

By adding $\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution to soluble bromide solution.

Properties: 

(i) It is insoluble in water and conc. acid but soluble in excess of strong solution of ammonia (e.g. $\text{AgCl}$ is soluble in dilute solution of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH},\text{AgI}$ is insoluble in $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}$ solution.

(ii) Silver halides, are also soluble in KCN and hypo solutions

(iii) On heating, it melts to red liquid.

(iv) It is used as the light sensitive material in photographic films. It is the most sensitive silver halide to photo-reduction.

concepts.title: Zinc oxide
concepts.conceptBody:
Compounds Of Zinc

Zinc Oxide – ZnO 

Preparation: 

It is prepared by burning zinc metal in air or by heating the zinc carbonate or zinc nitrate.

$2\text{Zn}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{ZnO}$

$\text{ZnC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ZnO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$2\text{Zn}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{ZnO}+4\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Properties: 

Zinc oxide is a white power and becomes yellow on heating and the colour disappears on colling.

(i) It dissolves readily in mineral acids and forms the corresponding salts with alkalies, it forms zincates${{\left[ \text{Zn}{{(\text{OH})}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}$

$\text{ZnO}+2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ZnC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{ZnO}+2\text{NaOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\left[ \text{Zn}{{(\text{OH})}_{4}} \right]$

(ii) It reduced to metallic zinc when heated with carbon or hydrogen

$\text{ZnO}+\text{C}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Zn}+\text{CO}$

$\text{ZnO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[{}]{}\text{Zn}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

concepts.title: Lead chloride and Lead Tetrachloride
concepts.conceptBody:
Chlorides of Lead

Lead chloride, $\mathbf{PbC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$ 

Preparation: 

Prepared by slow direct combination or by the action of boiling concentrated $\text{HCl}$ on lead (its oxide or carbonate).

$\text{Pb}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

$\text{Pb}+2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\uparrow $

$\text{PbO}+2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The usual method of preparation is to precipitate $\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ as a white crystalline precipitate by adding a soluble chloride to a lead salt solution.

$\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+2\text{NaCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+2\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

Properties: 

It is sparingly in cold water but more soluble in hot water. In concentrated solutions of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions, it dissolves forming complex ions, ${{\left[ \text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{3}} \right]}^{-}}$and ${{\left[ \text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}$.

Lead tetrachloride, $\mathbf{PbC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$ 

Properties: 

$\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ is a yellow only liquid. it is heavy and dissolves in organic solvents. It is a covalent and unstable compound, readily decomposes on heating.

$\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow $

It is easily hydrolysed by water and forms a double salt with $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}\text{. }$

$\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+4\text{HCl}$

Lead tetrabromide and tetraiodide are unknown, so the dihalides dominate the halogen chemistry of lead.

concepts.title: Red lead , Lead monoxide and dioxide
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxides Of Lead

Lead monoxide, PbO 

Preparation: 

$2~\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }2\text{PbO}+4\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

$\text{PbC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{PbO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

Dry air has no action on lead, but in moist air it tarnishes, forming a film of oxide first and finally basic carbonate, which protects it from further action. On heating in air or oxygen, it forms litharge, $\text{PbO}$. But prolonged heating gives red lead, $\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$.

$2~\text{Pb}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{PbO}$

$6\text{PbO}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2~\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

Properties: 

(i) At room temperature, it is a yellow amorphous powder that is insoluble in water but dissolves in acids as well as alkalies.

$\text{PbO}+2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{PbO}+2\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Thus, it behaves as an amphoteric oxide. The acetic properties being rather feeble.

(ii) It is easily reduced to the metallic state by hydrogen, carbon or carbon monoxide.

(iii) In the red form of$\text{PbO}$, the $\text{Pb}(\text{II})$ ions are four co-ordinate but the ${{\text{O}}^{2-}}$ ions around the $\text{Pb}(\text{II})$ lie in a square

Lead dioxide, $\mathbf{Pb}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$ 

Preparation: 

Action of cold concentrated nitric acid on red lead gives lead nitrate in solution while lead dioxide is thrown as a chocolate powder.

$\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\downarrow +2~\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Properties: 

(i) It is a chocolate coloured powder insoluble in water and dilute acids.

(ii) It liberates oxygen on gentle heating.

$2\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{PbO}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(iii) At 440 C, it is converted into red lead, $\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\text{. }$

$3\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{440{}^\circ C}\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(iv) $\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is an amphoteric oxide.

$\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+4\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\downarrow +\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow +2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$2\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{PbS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\downarrow +2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(v) It is a good oxidizing agent. It oxidizes manganous salts to pink permanganic acid when boiled in nitric acid solution.

$2\text{MnS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+5\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+6\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{PbS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+3~\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(vi) In alkaline medium, chromium hydroxide is oxdized to yellow chromate by$\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

$2\text{Cr}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}+10\text{KOH}+3\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+3~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+8{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The maroon form of lead(IV) oxide,$\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$, crystallizes in the rutile structure. This oxide is a component of the cathode of a lead-acid battery.

Red lead,$\mathbf{P}{{\mathbf{b}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$

Preparation: 

Roasting of litharge in air at $450\text{C}$ gives a bright red powder.

$6\text{PbO}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2~\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

It is also known as sindur.

Properties: 

Sparingly soluble in water but dissolves in dilute nitric acid.

$\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2~\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\downarrow +2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The above reaction indicates that red lead may be considered as plumbous ortho plumbate, 2PbO.PbO$_{2}$

concepts.title: Potassium Dichromate
concepts.conceptBody:
Potassium Dichromate $\left( {{\mathbf{K}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{C}{{\mathbf{r}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{7}}} \right)$

When chromite $\left[ \text{Fe}{{\left( \text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}} \right]$ is fused with $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ in air (or $\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ ) sodium chromate $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is formed.

$2\text{Fe}{{\left( \text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}+4\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }4\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

The fused mass is extracted with water and filtered. The filterate is concentrated and acidified with ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$. It produces $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$.

$2\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The sodium is concentrated and then saturated with KCl orange crystal of ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ separates out.

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+2\text{KCl}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+2\text{NaCl}$

Sodium dichromate is hygroscopic but not the ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$. Therefore, ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ is preferred over $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$.

Properties: 

(i) It is orange crystalline solid. Soluble in water, colour is due to charge transfer.

(ii) With alkali (i.e. $\text{pH}>7$) it turns yellow due to the formation of chromate.

${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+2\text{KOH}\xrightarrow{{}}2~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(iii) When${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ is heated it gives ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$4~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }4~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

But ${{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}\text{ on heating gives }{{\text{N}}_{2}}$

$\underset{\text{ (Orange) }}{\mathop{{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{7}}}}\,\xrightarrow{\Delta }\underset{\text{ (Green) }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{N}}_{2}}$

(iv) Solid${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ with conc. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ forms a red solution. The red is dichromic acid.

${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

When the solution is concentrated bright orange (or red) crystals of $\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ are formed.

$\text{ Red solution }\xrightarrow{Crytallisation}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

That is ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ is dehydrated by conc. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ to bright orange solid $\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$. The $\left( {{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}} \right.$ + conc. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ ) mixture is used for cleaning glassware as it is highly oxidizing.

(v) ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ as oxidizing agent

${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ is a good oxidizing agent in acid medium and reacts as

$\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}_{7}^{-2}+6\text{e}+14{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}2Cr_{\left( _{\left( green \right)}^{aq} \right)}^{3+}7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\quad \text{E}=1.36~\text{V}$

It oxidizes:

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}} \\ \left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S} \right)\quad & {{\text{S}}^{-2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{S} \\ {} & {{\text{I}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{I}}_{2}} \\ {} & \text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{SO}_{4}^{-2} \\ \end{array}$

Reactions:

$\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}_{7}^{-2}+6\text{e}+14{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{3+}}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \left. \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}+\text{e} \right]\times 6 \\ \overline{\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}_{7}^{-2}+6\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}+14{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{+3}}+6\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}} \\ \end{array}$

Thus $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$ turn orange solution of ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ (acidic) to green due to the reduction of $\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}_{7}^{-2}$ ion to $\text{Cr}_{(\text{aq})}^{+3}$.

(vi) ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ as qualitative reagent

To test ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}:{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ (or ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ ) in acid solution + amyl alcohol, when treated with ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$, $\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$ is formed. It dissolves in amyl alcohol making deep blue solution.

$\text{CrO}_{4}^{-2}+2{{\text{H}}^{+}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{5}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{CrO}_{7}^{-2}+2{{\text{H}}^{+}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{5}}+5{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{5}}+$ amyl alcohol $\xrightarrow{{}}$ deep blue

$\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$ is not stable in aq. medium and decomposes to $\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{+3}}$ and ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

$4\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{5}}+12{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{+3}}+7{{\text{O}}_{2}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(vii) ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}.$ as quantitative reagent

The acidic solution of ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is an oxidizing agent. The solution is a primary standard as:

(a) ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ prepared very pure

(b) Solution of ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}$ stable

(c) The solution is much less readily reduced by organic substances

(d) Not affected by light.

Structure of $\mathbf{C}{{\mathbf{r}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O}_{\mathbf{7}}^{\mathbf{-2}}$

The structure involves two tetrahedra joined through $\text{O}-$ atom. $\text{Cr}$ is sp $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{3}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}_{7}^{-2}$. The structure is

The Cr-O bonds have multiple bond nature due to (d-p) interaction.

concepts.title: Potassium Permagnate
concepts.conceptBody:
Potassium Permanganate $\left( \mathbf{KMn}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}} \right)$

When $\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (Pyrolusite) is fused with $\text{KOH}$ and $\text{KN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ (O.A.) ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ (green) is formed. It $\left( {{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)$ is oxidized by $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ to $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$.

$\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2\text{KOH}+\text{KN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow[(ii)\,\,water]{(i)\,\,fuse}{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{KN}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

\[2~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{KCl}\]

Oxidation of ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ may be caused electrolytically (better).

Anode reaction $2\text{MnO}_{4}^{-2}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

Cathode reaction $2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{H}{{\text{O}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

Oxidized solution on concentration gives violet (deep purple) crystals of $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ (The crystals however, appear almost black).

concepts.title: Reactions in Alkali and Alkaline Medim
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties of Potassium Permanganate: 

(i) Violet solid colour is due to charge transfer.

(ii) $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is soluble in water and the solution is red violet.

(iii) $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is widely used as a laboratory oxidizing agent. It is oxidant in neutral, acid and basic solutions. The reaction are:

(a) In acid medium

$\underset{\text{ (pink) }}{\mathop{\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}}}\,+5\text{e}+8{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{ (colourless) }}{\mathop{\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}}}\,+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O},\text{E}=1.54~\text{V}$

(b) Neutral or dil.alkali:

$\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+3\text{e}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{ (black solid) }}{\mathop{\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}\,+4\text{H}{{\text{O}}^{-}}$

(c) conc. alkali: $\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\text{e}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{MnO}_{4}^{-2}$

Hydrochloric acid $(\text{HCl})$ is not used to create acid medium as $\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}$oxidizes $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$to $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ $\left( \text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}-\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{E}=1.34~\text{V} \right).$

$\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+5\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}+8{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}+_{2}^{5}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

In acid medium $\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}$oxidizes,

${{\text{S}}^{-2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{S}$

$\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow[{}]{}\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}$

${{\text{I}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}$

$\text{NO}_{2}^{-}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NO}_{3}^{-}$

$\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{SO}_{4}^{-2}$

${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

${{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{O}_{4}^{-2}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

and itself is reduced to $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$. Therefore, pink solution of $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is decolourised in all of the above reactions. In the case of ${{\text{S}}^{-2}}$, white $\text{S}$ precipitates.

$\left. \text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+5\text{e}+8{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right]2$

$\begin{align} & \left. {{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{O}_{4}^{-2}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2\text{e} \right]5 \\ & \overline{2\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+16{{\text{H}}^{+}}+2{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{O}_{4}^{-2}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}+10\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+8{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}} \\ \end{align}$

Other reactions can be written in the same way.

$\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution (light green) decolourises $\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}$solution in acid medium. But if excess

$\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}$is added a black ppt. is formed. It is due to oxidation of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ to $\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

$\underset{\text{(pink)}}{\mathop{MnO_{4}^{-}}}\,+5F{{e}^{2+}}+8{{H}^{+}}\xrightarrow[{}]{acid}\underset{\text{(colourless)}}{\mathop{M{{n}^{+2}}}}\,+5F{{e}^{3+}}+4{{H}_{2}}O$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} M{{n}^{+2}}+2{{H}_{2}}O\to Mn{{O}_{2}}+2e+4{{H}^{+}}]3 \\ MnO_{4}^{-}+3e+4{{H}^{+}}\to Mn{{O}_{2}}+2{{H}_{2}}O]2 \\ 3M{{n}^{+2}}+2MnO_{4}^{-}+2{{H}_{2}}O\to 5Mn{{O}_{2}}+4{{H}^{+}} \\ \end{array}$

In alkaline solution (${{\text{I}}^{-}}$) is oxidized by $\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}$ to iodate $\left( \text{IO}_{3}^{-} \right)$.

${{\text{I}}^{-}}+6\text{H}{{\text{O}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{IO}_{3}^{-}+6\text{e}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\left. \text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+3\text{e}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+4\text{H}{{\text{O}}^{-}} \right]2$

$\overline{{{\text{I}}^{-}}+2\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{IO}_{3}^{-}+2\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}}$

(iv) $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ as volumetric reagent

Standard acidic solution of $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is oxidizing agent and is used as a volumetric reagent. But it is a secondary standard (not a primary standard like ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ ), because $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ converts to $\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ in water and oxidizes ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$. The reaction is, however, very slow.

$\left. \text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+3\text{e}+4{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right]2$

$\left. {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2{{\text{H}}^{+}}+2\text{e}{{+}^{1}}/2{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right]\times 3$

$\overline{2\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+2{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+3/2{{\text{O}}_{2}}}$

It is for the above reason; $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution in volumetric analysis is standardized before every use.

Titration with $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution does not require indicator. It acts as a self-indicator because even a drop of dil. $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ imparts pale pink colour to the solution.

It is used for the estimation of iron, ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}},\text{NO}_{2}^{-},\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (in pyrolusite) etc.
